title: Only 3 levers remain in UA: budget, creative, and conversion signal — and creative may disappear too
insight: Thomas Petit argues that with the full automation of media buying (UAC, Advantage+, PMax, Smart+), advertisers have been stripped down to only three meaningful levers: budget, creative assets, and conversion signal. He goes further to suggest that even creative may not remain a lever for long, citing Zuckerberg's May 2025 Stratechery interview where Meta envisions a future where advertisers only provide their objective and bank account — no creative, no targeting, no measurement needed. This makes signal engineering potentially the most durable competitive advantage in paid UA.
raw_excerpt: User Acquisition / media buying is now largely automated: UAC, (dis)Advantage+, Pmax, Smart+… Only 3 levers remaining: Budget, Creative assets, Conversion Signal. Even creatives may not be for long (!) — MZ: 'you tell us what your objective is, you connect to your bank account, you don't need any creative, you don't need any targeting demographic'
actionable_steps: Accept that traditional media buying levers (audiences, placements, bids) are being automated away — invest in signal and creative instead
Prioritize signal engineering as the most durable competitive moat since creative automation is accelerating
Audit which of the three levers (budget, creative, signal) you're currently underinvesting in
Track Meta/Google automation announcements — prepare for a future where creative is also automated

title: Evaluate signal quality using Shumel Lais' F1 score framework: balance precision and recall
insight: Shumel Lais (Day30) proposes using the F1 score — a machine learning metric combining precision and recall — to evaluate signal quality. Precision measures what fraction of users who triggered the signal actually became paying subscribers. Recall measures what fraction of actual subscribers were captured by the signal. Example from the presentation: 'Trial only' has 25% precision and 100% recall (F1=0.40), 'Trial + 1 content view' has 29% precision and 80% recall (F1=0.43), and 'Trial + 10 content views' has 33% precision but only 40% recall (F1=0.36). The sweet spot is the signal that maximizes F1 — not the most precise one, because too much filtering kills recall and starves the algorithm.
raw_excerpt: The best signal is the optimal balance of high precision & high recall for the minimum volume of signals needed. Metric #2: Precision = true positives / (true positives + false positives). Metric #3: Recall = true positives / (true positives + false negatives). Metrics #4: F1 Score = 2 x Precision x Recall / (Precision + Recall). Trial only: 100 volume, 25% precision, 100% recall, F1=0.40. Trial + 1 Content View: 70 volume, 29% precision, 80% recall, F1=0.43. Trial + 10 Content Views: 30 volume, 33% precision, 40% recall, F1=0.36.
actionable_steps: Calculate precision for each candidate signal: what % of users who triggered it became paying subscribers?
Calculate recall for each candidate signal: what % of actual paying subscribers triggered this signal?
Compute F1 score = 2 × Precision × Recall / (Precision + Recall) for each signal candidate
Choose the signal with the highest F1 score, not the highest precision — precision-only thinking starves the algorithm
Recompute F1 scores quarterly as your user base and conversion rates evolve

title: Use user persona filtering as a signal: optimize for 'small business owner trials' not 'all trials'
insight: Thomas Petit shares a case study from an unnamed AI app where the highest-value users were business owners. Normally 40-50% of onboarding completers self-identified as small business owners and showed much higher ARPU. However, a new winning Meta creative was attracting mostly non-business users — only 10% business owners despite good surface CPT metrics. By switching the optimization event from 'all trials' to 'small business owner trials only' (firing the event only when users selected that persona in onboarding), the business owner share jumped from 10% to 35%, with a 50% ROAS improvement. This is a concrete implementation of the persona-based qualified trial concept.
raw_excerpt: Unnamed AI app. High value users: business owners. Usually 40-50% of users completing onboarding self-qualify as 'small business owner', showing much higher ARPU. Problem: new winning creative on Meta getting traction, even bringing decent CPT & ROAS, but only with 10% 'biz owners'. Meta targeting too broad, missing on the core audience. => moved from 'all trials' to 'small business owner trials only' as the optimization event. Result: from 10% to 35% business owners, +50% ROAS.
actionable_steps: Identify which onboarding persona/answer correlates most strongly with high ARPU or LTV
Create a custom event that only fires when the high-value persona is selected during onboarding
Map this filtered event to a Meta standard event (e.g., Purchase or Subscribe) for optimization
Monitor the persona distribution in your acquired cohorts — if it drifts below target, your creative may be attracting the wrong audience
Don't use this if your high-value persona is less than 20% of trials — you'll starve the algorithm

title: Signal engineering at low scale: switch to higher-funnel proxy events when trial volume is below 10/day
insight: Thomas Petit shares a case study from tonestro (music learning app, Christoph Huber). The app had fewer than 10 trial starts per day, far below Meta's learning threshold. The solution was threefold: switch optimization to a higher-volume proxy ('Onboarding Completed'), map the custom quality signal to Meta's standard Purchase event, and feed the same signal to Google UAC on Android. Result: daily event volume exceeded Meta's 50/week guideline, cost per trial dropped ~48% initially, and after further iterations with the same creatives, cost per trial fell by approximately 64%. This demonstrates that at low scale, going higher in funnel with a quality proxy is better than optimizing for a rare event.
raw_excerpt: tonestro: Learn to play music. The problem: not enough 'Trial Start' per day (<10). The change: Switched the optimization event to a higher-volume proxy: 'Onboarding Completed', mapped his custom quality signal to Meta standard Purchase event, Fed the same signal to Google UAC (Android). Outcome: Boosted daily event volume well beyond Meta's 50/week guideline. Early test result: Cost per trial ↓ ~48%. After further iterations (same creatives): Cost per trial fell by ≈64%.
actionable_steps: If trial starts are below 10/day/campaign, switch optimization to a higher-volume proxy like 'onboarding_completed'
Map your quality signal to a Meta standard event (Purchase preferred) rather than a custom event for better algorithm treatment
Feed the same quality signal to Google UAC on Android to help that algorithm find good users earlier
After volume stabilizes above 50/week, consider gradually moving back down-funnel to a more qualified event
Track the proxy event's correlation with actual trial-to-paid conversion to ensure the proxy remains meaningful

title: Going too far down funnel causes audience saturation and CAC spikes — watch for the ceiling
insight: Thomas Petit warns about a common failure mode: combining a narrowly-targeted creative with a highly-filtered conversion event can cause creative and signal to steer targeting in opposite directions, resulting in an audience that's too small. He shows a real case (anonymized) with three phases: (1) new winning creative with all-trials optimization scales spend but at high CAC, (2) switching to qualified trials dramatically drops CAC and allows further scaling, (3) eventually hits a ceiling where audience saturation causes CAC to spike again. The key insight is that signal engineering at the extreme creates diminishing returns because you narrow the targetable audience below the platform's ability to find users efficiently.
raw_excerpt: Be careful going too far down funnel… Creatives & event steering in opposite direction = lower audience size, confusing signal. Scale issue! 1: New winner x all trials: scale up but high CAC. 2: Winner x qualified trials: scale way up low CAC. 3: Hits a ceiling, audience saturation, CAC increases.
actionable_steps: Monitor the ratio between ad spend and CAC — if CAC starts rising while spend is flat, you may be hitting audience saturation
Don't combine a narrow creative angle with a highly-filtered event — they compound each other's audience restrictions
When you hit a ceiling on qualified trials, consider running parallel campaigns: one on qualified trials, one on broader all-trials with different creatives
Track audience reach frequency — rising frequency with flat/declining results signals saturation
Have a 'broadening' plan ready: when qualified trials hit ceiling, widen the signal or add new creative angles to expand reach

title: Use Value Rules for age-based bid suppression instead of filtering young users from events
insight: Thomas Petit highlights that age-based quality variance (young users start many trials but rarely convert to paid) is one of the most common signal engineering problems, but the solution shouldn't always be event filtering. Three common approaches all have drawbacks: optimizing for all trials ignores the quality gap, excluding young users from targeting kills reach and raises CPMs, and filtering young users from the conversion event still largely excludes them. The recommended solution on Meta is to use Value Rules (bid modifiers): for example, assign users under 25 a -30% bid modification. This keeps targeting broad (maintaining CPM efficiency) while giving Meta a more accurate picture of actual user value by demographic. The limitation is that Value Rules are currently only available on Meta Ads.
raw_excerpt: A very common trial value criteria: user age. Solutions: Aim at all trials, get cheap CPT — Problem: don't end up generating value. Filter out users under X years old in targeting — Problem: fully exclude age range lowers reach & increase CPM. Filter out users under X years old in events & creatives — Problem: still largely exclude age. Solution: Use bid modifiers/value-based rules: Ex: assign <25yo a -30%. => Enables to keep targeting broad & CPM lower while telling the platform a closer picture of your reality. Problem: only available on Meta Ads.
actionable_steps: Analyze trial-to-paid conversion by age bracket to quantify the value gap between demographics
Instead of excluding young users from events, use Meta's Value Rules to assign a negative bid modifier (e.g., -30% for under 25)
Set up Value Rules in Meta Advertising Settings: select the age dimension, set % deviation from average based on actual conversion data
Keep targeting broad to maintain lower CPMs and broader reach — let Value Rules handle demographic weighting
Combine Value Rules with Burke's approach: use multiple value dimensions (age, placement, OS) simultaneously

title: Eric Seufert's 'tail wagging the dog': build product friction specifically to generate intent signals
insight: Thomas Petit presents Eric Seufert's concept that the most advanced form of signal engineering is building entirely new product experiences (screens, quizzes, hurdles) solely to create measurable intent signals — not because they improve the user experience, but because they help the ad platform find the right users. Seufert calls this 'the tail wagging the dog': it's a product exercise pursued exclusively for marketing performance. A workout app example optimizes not for quiz completion, but for a specific answer in their quiz funnel — which is highly correlated with purchasing. The idea extends to creating deliberate challenges or friction points that test user willingness to proceed, serving as proxies for purchase intent. These frictions must be explicitly engineered and tested for correlation with value — standard onboardings designed to reduce friction will by definition fail to surface these intent signals.
raw_excerpt: I characterize Signal Engineering as 'the tail wagging the dog' because, conceptually, it involves *creating* new opportunities to measure intent in the product experience. It's a product exercise pursued exclusively for the purpose of improving marketing performance. For this reason, it generally requires introducing new, deliberate points of friction into the product experience (often during onboarding) to test the user's willingness to proceed. — Eric Seufert
actionable_steps: Audit your onboarding: does any step currently generate a measurable signal that correlates with purchase intent?
Consider adding a deliberate 'friction' step — a quiz question, a mini-challenge, or an engagement hurdle — specifically to create a high-intent event
Test the new friction point against actual downstream conversion: does passing this hurdle predict purchasing significantly better than the baseline?
Optimize ad campaigns toward this engineered top-funnel signal rather than trials or purchases — it gives the algorithm broader reach while maintaining quality
Iterate: the first friction you design may not correlate well. This is an experimentation process, not a one-shot implementation.

title: Value-based campaigns need variance — sending average values is worse than not using VBO at all
insight: Thomas Petit emphasizes that value-based optimization (VO/VBB/tROAS) campaigns are powerful for gaming and e-commerce due to natural purchase value variance, but most subscription businesses don't have enough value variance to make them work. If all users have roughly the same subscription price, sending value signals is useless because the algorithm can't differentiate between user segments. The worst thing you can do is send average values. To make VBO work for subscription apps, you must engineer variance: either through predicted LTV that meaningfully differs across segments, or by artificially amplifying the spread. Thomas mentions he sometimes artificially inflates high-value customer values, while a gaming company caps whale values — both valid depending on whether you want the algorithm to chase more high-value users or find more 'medium-value' ones at scale.
raw_excerpt: Value needs variance – Most subscriptions business don't have enough of it. The reality of your userbase and what algorithm need can conflict. Useless without variance: the worst you can do is send average. I sometimes fake increase the value of my highest value customers. A gaming company does the opposite, capping the value of its whales.
actionable_steps: Before launching VBO campaigns, check if your user value distribution has meaningful variance — if 80% of users are within 20% of the mean, VBO won't help
Engineer variance by incorporating predicted LTV based on onboarding answers, device, age, and behavior signals
Consider amplifying high-value users (inflate their value by a multiplier) to create enough spread for the algorithm to differentiate
For gaming: consider capping whale values to prevent the algorithm from over-optimizing for rare outliers at the expense of profitable mid-tier users
Run both AEO (event-based) and VBO campaigns in parallel — they target different user profiles and can expand total efficient reach

title: Measure signal engineering success with ROAS (LTV/CAC), not with CAC alone — costs will rise
insight: Thomas Petit warns that CPM, CPI, cost per trial, and even CAC can all increase when implementing signal engineering — and that's expected. Higher-quality audiences are smaller, harder to reach, and more expensive to acquire. The correct measurement is whether LTV increases more than CAC does, resulting in net ROAS improvement. He also warns against using predicted/modeled LTV to assess signal engineering changes, because cohorts acquired through different signals won't behave the same as historical cohorts — your pLTV model was trained on old data and may not generalize to the new audience mix. You need actual observed cohort data to validate.
raw_excerpt: CPM, CPI, Cost per trial & even CAC can increase. That's expected. Valuable audiences are smaller, harder to reach, more expensive. What you want to look at: LTV increasing more than CAC (or ARPT more than CPT). Don't use modelled/predictive to assess! Cohorts coming from different signal won't behave the same.
actionable_steps: Do NOT evaluate signal engineering changes by looking at CAC alone — higher CAC is expected and acceptable
Track the LTV/CAC ratio or ARPT/CPT ratio: the signal change is working if LTV grows faster than CAC
Wait for actual observed cohort data (D30, D60, D90) before declaring success — don't rely on pLTV models for new signal cohorts
Set up cohort tagging by signal type so you can compare LTV curves between 'all trials' vs 'qualified trials' cohorts
Accept that initial CPM increases are the cost of reaching higher-quality users — the payback comes in retention and renewal rates

title: Run different signals in parallel across campaigns to broaden reach, not just improve ROAS
insight: Thomas Petit makes the case that signal engineering isn't only about improving ROAS — it can also be used to expand the total addressable audience by running different conversion events in parallel campaigns. Different signals attract different user profiles. He cites Marcus Burke's recommendation that having multiple events in different campaigns broadens who you're acquiring and addresses a bigger market: some users convert and monetize quickly, others develop value over the long run. This parallels Burke's deconsolidation philosophy applied to signal strategy. The slide also references Cody Plofker's statement that Cost per 1k Accounts Reached (CPMr) is the most important Meta KPI for scale — suggesting that reach efficiency matters as much as conversion efficiency at 9-figure levels.
raw_excerpt: It's not all about ROAS… Reach, target groups, user persona: signal engineering can be used not just to improve ROAS, but expand the audience. Use different signals in parallel in different campaigns to broaden targeting. 'There can be value in having multiple events different campaigns optimize for to broaden who you're acquiring and address a bigger market.' — Marcus Burke (29:32)
actionable_steps: Don't use a single optimization event across your entire account — run different events in separate campaigns to reach different user profiles
Example structure: one campaign optimizing for 'qualified trial' (highest LTV users), another on 'all trials' (broader reach), another on 'purchase' (direct subscribers)
Track which signal/campaign combos reach genuinely different audiences by comparing demographic and placement breakdowns
Use this as a scaling strategy: when your qualified-trial campaign hits a ceiling, the broader-signal campaigns maintain volume growth
Monitor CPMr (cost per 1K accounts reached) alongside ROAS to ensure you're not just optimizing the same shrinking audience

title: Anas Maqsood's 4-step signal upgrade: redefine conversion, attach predicted value, delay postback, include negative signals
insight: Thomas Petit highlights Anas Maqsood's LinkedIn case study as a practical signal engineering playbook with four concrete steps. Step 1: Redefine what counts as a conversion — move from 'install or trial started' to 'qualified trial' (users who meet specific behavioral criteria). Step 2: Attach a predicted value — instead of passing the actual subscription price, bucket users into expected 30-day LTV ranges ($0-10, $11-50, $51+) so value-based bidding can rank users by expected return. Step 3: Delay the postback by a few minutes to capture Day-0 behavior (session depth, first action) while staying well inside the attribution window — this provides richer context for value modeling. Step 4: Include negative signals — when a trial cancels early or a refund happens, send a 'Churn' event to steer the algorithm away from low-quality lookalikes. Results: Qualified Trial ROAS +31%, CPM down ~14%, stabilized delivery at lower bids.
raw_excerpt: 1. Redefine the 'conversion': From Install or Trial Started → To: Qualified Trial. Smaller volume? Yes. Clearer signal? Absolutely. Learning phase stabilised in ~6 days instead of 10+. 2. Attach a predicted value: bucketed users into expected 30-day LTV ranges ($0-10, $11-50, $51+). 3. Delay the Postback (But Stay In Window): delay the event a few minutes to capture Day-0 behaviour. 4. Include negative signals: send a 'Churn' event. Early results: Qualified Trial ROAS +31%, CPM down ~14%.
actionable_steps: Step 1: Switch optimization from 'any trial' to a qualified trial event that filters for behavioral criteria (e.g., completed onboarding + 1 meaningful action)
Step 2: Instead of passing subscription price as value, compute expected 30-day LTV buckets and pass those as the event value
Step 3: Delay the postback by a few minutes after trial start to capture first-session behavior data — but keep within 24-hour attribution window
Step 4: Send a negative 'Churn' or 'Refund' event via CAPI when trial users cancel early, to train the algorithm on who NOT to target
Track Qualified Trial ROAS as the primary success metric, not raw trial volume or CPT

title: Raw pLTV predictions alone don't move ad platform algorithms — they need signal engineering translation
insight: Thomas Petit cites Itai Kafri (Voyantis) warning that sending raw predicted LTV values directly to ad platforms often doesn't produce the expected optimization improvement. The ad platform's learning logic has specific requirements: events need to be timely, shaped in a format the platform understands, and calibrated to the platform's bidding system. A raw pLTV score of $47.32 means nothing to Meta's algorithm without proper translation into an event-shaped, appropriately-timed, variance-rich signal. Voyantis calls this the 'prescription layer' — the missing link between having good predictions and actually influencing ad network delivery. Thomas hints that one major platform (censored) will soon have dedicated pLTV optimization campaign types, separate from traditional VO campaigns.
raw_excerpt: 'Predictions without translation are noise. You can't just send a raw prediction to an ad platform and expect results. Signal Engineering turns predictions into event-shaped, timely feedback the platforms can actually learn from. Success in value-based optimization goes beyond simply sending accurate LTV predictions, and requires also a prescription layer to blend and translate these predictive models into actionable signals that effectively influence ad network algorithms.' — Itai Kafri, Voyantis
actionable_steps: Don't pipe raw pLTV scores directly into CAPI or value events — the algorithm won't know what to do with continuous predictions
Translate predictions into discrete value buckets or event-shaped signals that the platform's bidding system can learn from
Ensure predicted values are sent within the first 24 hours (ideally first session) so the platform can use them for optimization
If using a pLTV vendor (Voyantis, Churney, Day30), verify they have a 'prescription layer' that translates predictions into platform-optimized signals
Watch for new dedicated pLTV campaign types from major ad platforms — these may handle the translation natively

title: Signal engineering expected results: 10-50% ROAS improvement in 60-90 days from pLTV vendors
insight: Thomas Petit presents benchmark results from Churney and Voyantis showing the expected impact of signal engineering with predicted value. Churney reports 10-50% improvement in ROAS within 60 to 90 days across fitness app clients (Asana Rebel, Leadtech, Genesis group), with an average D60 ROAS lift of 27%. Perlego case showed +11.5% increase in 3-month ROAS and +18% month-3 retention. Voyantis case studies show Rappi achieving +33% ROAS with +30% paying customers, and MoneyLion achieving +48% ROAS increase. Importantly, the further along the automation journey (Advantage+/VO/PMax/tROAS), the better the signal engineering results — converting from simple tCPA goals requires more intermediate steps.
raw_excerpt: Churney: Advertisers typically experience a 10-50% improvement in ROAS within 60 to 90 days. Minimum benchmark for success is 10% ROAS uplift. Fitness apps: Average lift in D60 ROAS: 27%. 'The further you are in the journey to Adv+/VO/Pmax/rROAS, the better the results are.' Voyantis: Rappi +33% ROAS, +30% paying customers. MoneyLion +48% ROAS, +67% Activator ARPU.
source_author: Thomas Petit (citing Churney and Voyantis case studies)
actionable_steps: Set realistic expectations: 10-50% ROAS improvement in 60-90 days is the benchmark range from vendor case studies
Start with AEO + qualified trials before jumping to pLTV-powered VBO — results compound as you progress through the automation maturity curve
Evaluate pLTV vendors (Churney, Voyantis, Day30) against a minimum 10% ROAS uplift benchmark within 90 days
Consider that vendor case studies have survivorship bias — results depend heavily on your existing data quality and value variance
If you're still on basic tCPA campaigns, first migrate to Advantage+/VO before layering on signal engineering for maximum impact
